summary: Victorian English poet best known for dramatic monologues. Most commonly identified by "My Last Duchess," "Porphyria's Lover," and the collections Men and Women and Dramatis Personae. Married to Elizabeth Barrett Browning. Inspired Ezra Pound.

works.description: Dramatic monologue about the Duke of Ferrara describing a portrait of his deceased wife painted by Fra Pandolf.

works.name: Pippa Passes
works.description: Verse drama set in Asolo, Italy, about a girl whose song unknowingly affects those around her. Part of the Bells and Pomegranates series.

clues.clue: "God's in his heaven, all's right with the world" is from a song in this work
examples: "God's in his heaven, all's right with the world!"

clues.clue: Browning mistakenly used the word "twat" thinking it meant part of a nun's habit
examples: mistakenly thought that a vulgar word for a woman's genitals actually referred to part of a nun's habit

clues.clue: Pippa walks through Asolo and unknowingly saves Luigi from assassination

clues.clue: Parodied in Nabokov's Pale Fire ("Here Papa pisses")

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Browning is a Victorian English poet, primarily identified through his dramatic monologues. His most frequently clued poem is "My Last Duchess," in which the Duke of Ferrara describes a painting by Fra Pandolf of his deceased wife, who had a heart "too soon made glad." The poem ends with the Duke showing the emissary a bronze statue of Neptune cast by Claus of Innsbruck. "Porphyria's Lover" features a speaker who strangles his beloved with her yellow hair and notes that God "has not said a word." Pippa Passes is a verse drama set in Asolo containing the famous line "God's in his heaven, all's right with the world," and was notably parodied in Nabokov's Pale Fire.

Browning's "Soliloquy of the Spanish Cloister" opens with "Gr-r-r" and features a monk plotting against Brother Lawrence who drinks orange juice in three sips to frustrate the Arian. "Rabbi Ben Ezra" begins "Grow old along with me! The best is yet to be" and responds to the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam. "Andrea del Sarto" contains the line "a man's reach should exceed his grasp."

The Ring and the Book is an epic based on a 1698 Roman murder trial. "Childe Roland to the Dark Tower Came" takes its title from King Lear and ends with the slug-horn being blown. Sordello is a notoriously difficult poem about a troubadour that prompted Ezra Pound to write "Hang it all, Robert Browning, there can be but one Sordello" in Canto II.

Browning was married to Elizabeth Barrett Browning and lived with her at the Casa Guidi in Italy. He inspired Ezra Pound and is the basis for Randolph Henry Ash in A.S. Byatt's Possession. His collection Men and Women contains many of his best-known monologues. Other notable poems include "The Lost Leader" (attacking Wordsworth), "The Bishop Orders His Tomb," "Two in the Campagna," "A Toccata of Galuppi's," and "Caliban Upon Setebos."

cards.front: Poem: the speaker describes a painting by Fra Pandolf of his deceased wife, who had a heart "too soon made glad"
Poem: the emissary is shown a bronze statue of Neptune cast by Claus of Innsbruck
Poem: the speaker strangles his beloved with her own yellow hair, and the poem ends "And yet God has not said a word!"
Play: contains the song "God's in his heaven, all's right with the world" sung by a girl walking through Asolo
Play: the author mistakenly used the word "twat" thinking it meant part of a nun's habit in this verse drama
Poem: opens "Gr-r-r" and features a monk who drinks orange juice in three sips to frustrate the Arian while plotting against Brother Lawrence
cards.back: Soliloquy of the Spanish Cloister (Robert Browning)

cards.front: Poem: begins "Grow old along with me! The best is yet to be" and responds to the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam
Poem: epic based on a 1698 Roman murder trial of Guido Franceschini before Pope Innocent XII
cards.back: The Ring and the Book (Robert Browning)

cards.front: Poem: title taken from King Lear; ends with the knight blowing the "slug-horn" upon reaching the Dark Tower
cards.back: Childe Roland to the Dark Tower Came (Robert Browning)
cards.work: Childe Roland to the Dark Tower Came

cards.front: Poem: the speaker tells Lucrezia "a man's reach should exceed his grasp, / Or what's a heaven for?"
Poem: Ezra Pound opens Canto II with "Hang it all... there can be but one" of this notoriously difficult poem about a troubadour from Mantua
Poem: attacks Wordsworth with "Just for a handful of silver he left us / Just for a riband to stick in his coat"
Poem: the bishop wants "peach-blossom marble" and "nine columns" to outdo "Old Gandolf with his paltry onion stone"
cards.back: The Bishop Orders His Tomb at Saint Praxed's Church (Robert Browning)

cards.front: Author: A.S. Byatt modeled the Victorian poet Randolph Henry Ash on this real poet in her novel Possession
Poem: the speaker feels "chilly and grown old" after "lesser thirds" and "sixths diminished" evoke thoughts of 18th-century Venice
Poem: laments "Infinite passion, and the pain / Of finite hearts that yearn" while set in the Italian countryside
Author: exposed the fraudulence of medium Daniel Dunglas Home in the mocking poem "Mr. Sludge, the Medium"
Poem: the title alchemist converses with the Italian poet Aprile, who says "God is the perfect poet"
